title: Multithreading & Concurrency
description: Python concurrency is the single most common topic in FAANG mid-to-senior Python interviews. You must understand the GIL, when threading helps despite it, asyncio vs threading decision frameworks, and the no-GIL (free-threading) future in Python 3.13+.

sections.title: 1. Multithreading — The GIL Tax (I/O Wins, CPU Loses)
sections.description: Threads share process memory and are lightweight. CPython's GIL (Global Interpreter Lock) allows only one thread to execute Python bytecode at a time. Threads DO speed up I/O-bound work because the GIL is released during blocking syscalls (time.sleep(), socket.recv(), file reads). They DON'T help CPU-bound work. This is the first question any FAANG interviewer will ask about Python concurrency.
sections.codeBlock:
import threading
import time

def fetch_api_data(endpoint):
    print(f"[Thread-{threading.current_thread().name}] Fetching {endpoint}...")
    time.sleep(1) # Simulates network blocking I/O (GIL is released here!)
    print(f"[Thread-{threading.current_thread().name}] Finished {endpoint}")

threads = []
for i, url in enumerate(["/users", "/posts", "/comments"]):
    t = threading.Thread(target=fetch_api_data, args=(url,), name=str(i+1))
    threads.append(t)
    t.start()

for t in threads:
    t.join()

sections.title: 2. Multiprocessing — True Parallelism for CPU-Bound Work
sections.description: Each process gets its own GIL. No lock contention across cores. Use multiprocessing for CPU-bound compute or when you need to bypass the GIL entirely. The overhead is higher (process creation, IPC via Queue/Pipe/shared memory). FAANG interviewers will ask you to compare this to threading — know the numbers: thread creation ~50 microseconds, process creation ~1-5 milliseconds.

sections.title: 3. Asyncio — 10K Connections on One Thread
sections.description: Asyncio uses cooperative multitasking on a single thread. The event loop switches between coroutines at await points. Zero thread-switching overhead — can handle 10,000+ concurrent connections. The catch: any blocking call (time.sleep(), requests.get(), CPU-bound loop) blocks the ENTIRE event loop. FAANG interviewers love asking: 'When would you choose asyncio over threading?' — answer: when you have many concurrent I/O channels and want to avoid thread overhead; threading when you need preemptive scheduling or interface with blocking C extensions.
sections.codeBlock:
import asyncio

async def fetch_async(service_id):
    print(f"[Async] Call start to service {service_id}")
    await asyncio.sleep(1.5)  # Cooperative sleep yields execution back to Event Loop
    print(f"[Async] Call finish for service {service_id}")
    return f"result_{service_id}"

async def main():
    results = await asyncio.gather(
        fetch_async(1),
        fetch_async(2),
        fetch_async(3)
    )
    print("All API calls finished successfully:", results)

# Running the coroutine
try:
    # Standard Python script execution
    asyncio.run(main())
except RuntimeError:
    # Jupyter Notebook context (running inside an active event loop)
    await main()

sections.title: 4. GIL Deep Dive — What, Why, and Python 3.13+ No-GIL
sections.description:
The GIL is a mutex that protects CPython's internals from race conditions. It simplifies C extension development and makes single-threaded code faster (no per-object locking overhead). But it kills multi-core parallelism for Python threads.

What releases the GIL? I/O operations, C extensions that explicitly release it (NumPy, pandas), time.sleep(). What holds it? Any Python bytecode execution — loops, arithmetic, dict lookups.

Python 3.13 free-threading (no-GIL mode): Activated via PYTHON_GIL=0 or configure flag. Per-object reference counting + biased reference counting replaces the global lock. Atomics for thread-safe operations. May be default in Python 4.0. Interview angle: 'How would you write thread-safe code that works under both GIL and no-GIL?' — still use locks; the GIL was never a correctness guarantee.
sections.codeBlock:
import sys, threading, time

# GIL demo: CPU-bound threads are SEQUENTIAL on CPython
def count_down(n):
    while n > 0: n -= 1  # pure Python; GIL never released

# Two threads on CPU-bound work run NO faster than one
start = time.perf_counter()
t1 = threading.Thread(target=count_down, args=(50_000_000,))
t2 = threading.Thread(target=count_down, args=(50_000_000,))
t1.start(); t2.start()
t1.join(); t2.join()
print(f'Thread time: {time.perf_counter() - start:.2f}s')
# ≈ same as running 100M sequentially — GIL serializes

# Compare with multiprocessing:
import multiprocessing
start = time.perf_counter()
p1 = multiprocessing.Process(target=count_down, args=(50_000_000,))
p2 = multiprocessing.Process(target=count_down, args=(50_000_000,))
p1.start(); p2.start()
p1.join(); p2.join()
print(f'Process time: {time.perf_counter() - start:.2f}s')
# ≈ 2x faster on a 2+ core machine

# Check no-GIL mode (Python 3.13+)
if hasattr(sys, '_is_gil_enabled'):
    print('GIL enabled:', sys._is_gil_enabled())  # False in no-GIL mode

sections.title: 5. Thread Safety — Locks, Deadlocks, and Correctness
sections.description:
The GIL does NOT make Python thread-safe. Two threads can still interleave at bytecode boundaries. The classic example: x += 1 is three bytecode ops (LOAD, ADD, STORE) — a context switch in the middle loses an increment. Always use locks when sharing mutable state.

Lock types: threading.Lock (mutex, not reentrant), RLock (reentrant — same thread can acquire again), Semaphore (n concurrent accesses), Condition (producer-consumer), Barrier (sync N threads).

Deadlock prevention: Always acquire locks in the same order. Use with lock: context managers. Know the dining philosophers problem — it's a common system design warm-up.
sections.codeBlock:
import threading

# The GIL does NOT protect this — lost update bug
counter = 0
def increment():
    global counter
    for _ in range(100_000):
        # LOAD counter, ADD 1, STORE counter — three bytecodes
        counter += 1  # NOT atomic!

threads = [threading.Thread(target=increment) for _ in range(10)]
for t in threads: t.start()
for t in threads: t.join()
print(f'Expected: 1,000,000, Got: {counter}')  # varies, < 1M

# Fix with Lock
lock = threading.Lock()
counter = 0
def safe_increment():
    global counter
    for _ in range(100_000):
        with lock:  # context manager — always releases
            counter += 1

threads = [threading.Thread(target=safe_increment) for _ in range(10)]
for t in threads: t.start()
for t in threads: t.join()
print(f'Expected: 1,000,000, Got: {counter}')  # 1,000,000

# Deadlock example — always lock in consistent order
lock_a = threading.Lock()
lock_b = threading.Lock()

def task_1():
    with lock_a:
        with lock_b: pass  # OK

def task_2_wrong():
    with lock_b:
        with lock_a: pass  # DEADLOCK if task_1 holds lock_a and task_2 holds lock_b

# Fix: same order everywhere
def task_2_right():
    with lock_a:           # same order as task_1
        with lock_b: pass

sections.title: 6. Concurrency Decision Framework — FAANG Interview Cheatsheet
sections.description:
The single most common Python concurrency interview question: 'I have X workload — should I use threads, processes, asyncio, or something else?' Here's the decision tree:

CPU-bound (matrix multiplication, image processing, simulation): multiprocessing or NumPy/Cython. Threading will NOT help because the GIL serializes execution.

I/O-bound, many connections (web server, API gateway, chat): asyncio. Zero thread overhead, 10K+ connections per process. Use aiohttp, httpx, asyncpg.

I/O-bound, few connections, existing blocking code (script that reads 3 files + calls 2 APIs): threading. Simple, no code changes needed. ThreadPoolExecutor is the easiest path.

Mixed workload (I/O + CPU): asyncio for I/O, run CPU work in a ProcessPoolExecutor via loop.run_in_executor().

FAANG interviewer follow-up: 'What about performance? How many requests per second?' — Know: asyncio + uvloop ~ 40K req/s; threading ~ 5-10K req/s; synchronous ~500 req/s (typical Python web numbers).
sections.codeBlock:
import asyncio
from concurrent.futures import ThreadPoolExecutor, ProcessPoolExecutor
import time

# === I/O-bound: ThreadPoolExecutor (simple) ===
def fetch_url(url):
    # blocking I/O — GIL released during socket ops
    # use requests, urllib, etc.
    return f'data from {url}'

with ThreadPoolExecutor(max_workers=8) as pool:
    results = list(pool.map(fetch_url, [f'https://api.example.com/{i}' for i in range(20)]))

# === I/O-bound: Asyncio (scalable) ===
import httpx
async def fetch_async(url):
    async with httpx.AsyncClient() as client:
        resp = await client.get(url)
        return resp.text

async def main():
    tasks = [fetch_async(f'https://api.example.com/{i}') for i in range(20)]
    return await asyncio.gather(*tasks)

results = asyncio.run(main())

# === Mixed: asyncio + ProcessPoolExecutor for CPU work ===
def cpu_intensive(data):
    # This runs in a separate process — bypasses GIL
    return sum(i * i for i in range(data))

async def mixed_workload():
    loop = asyncio.get_running_loop()
    with ProcessPoolExecutor() as pool:
        # CPU work runs in parallel processes while I/O runs on event loop
        cpu_result = await loop.run_in_executor(pool, cpu_intensive, 10_000_000)
        io_result = await fetch_async('https://api.example.com/data')
        return cpu_result, io_result

sections.title: References & Further Reading
sections.description:
• asyncio — Asynchronous I/O — Official Python async documentation
  • threading — Thread-based parallelism — Python threading module docs
  • multiprocessing — Process-based parallelism — Python multiprocessing module docs
  • Python Concurrency: A Guide — Real Python comparing threading, asyncio, and multiprocessing
  • What is the GIL? — Classic StackOverflow deep dive on the Global Interpreter Lock
  • Python Cookbook (3rd ed.) — Chapter 12: concurrency patterns for interview prep

diffTable.concurrency: The broad conceptual design structure of managing and interleaving multiple execution tasks asynchronously or concurrently over overlapping periods of time.
diffTable.multithreading: A specific technical implementation paradigm of concurrency where a single process spawns multiple execution context threads to compute tasks.

diffTable.concurrency: Focuses on task structure, organization, responsiveness, and asynchronous cooperative yielding to maximize performance efficiency.
diffTable.multithreading: Focuses on overlapping system CPU thread time-slices, context-switching execution, and running threads within a single shared memory block.

diffTable.concurrency: Can manage tasks without sharing state (e.g. via message queues or isolated independent OS processes).
diffTable.multithreading: All spawned threads share the exact same process memory space, descriptors, heap, and static variables.

diffTable.concurrency: Can be implemented via Multiprocessing, Asynchronous Event Loops (Asyncio), or Multithreading.
diffTable.multithreading: Utilizes preemptive OS-level kernel-managed thread allocations within the scheduler.

diffTable.concurrency: Depends on the underlying model (Multiprocessing is safe from shared-memory corruption; Asyncio is safe from thread race states).
diffTable.multithreading: Highly prone to concurrency corruption race conditions, deadlocks, and memory collision hazards if lock controls are missing.

details:
FAANG Concurrency Interview Workflow: (1) Identify the bottleneck — CPU, I/O, or mixed. (2) Choose the right model — threading for simple I/O, asyncio for high-concurrency I/O, multiprocessing for CPU, or a hybrid. (3) Discuss the GIL — always mention it before the interviewer asks. (4) Address thread safety — use locks, explain queue.Queue for producer-consumer, or use immutable data. (5) Name Python 3.13+ free-threading as the future direction.

Key numbers for interviews: Thread creation ~50us, process creation ~1-5ms, asyncio task creation ~0.5us. Context switch: thread ~10us, asyncio coroutine switch ~0.1us. Max concurrency: threading ~1K threads, asyncio ~10K-100K tasks, multiprocessing ~CPU core count. GIL is released during: I/O, sleep, C extension calls (NumPy, pandas), threading.Event.wait(), queue.Queue.get().

Common FAANG questions: How does Python GIL impact a web server? — threading handles concurrent requests by releasing GIL during I/O; CPU-heavy endpoints block all other requests. How would you make x += 1 thread-safe? — use threading.Lock or queue.Queue. What happens when you call time.sleep() in a thread? — GIL is released, other threads can run. How does asyncio handle 10,000 connections? — single-threaded event loop, cooperative multitasking, no context-switch overhead.

No-GIL future (Python 3.13+): Free-threaded builds remove the GIL. Multi-threaded CPU-bound code gets faster, but single-threaded code may be 5-10% slower due to per-object reference counting overhead. C extensions that relied on the GIL for protection need explicit locking. Mention this in FAANG interviews as the direction of Python, but note most production deployments still use the GIL (default mode).

References: Python Concurrency Docs — Asyncio Docs — PEP 703 (no-GIL).
